moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim to the central e5 and c5 squares while preparing to develop our pieces harmoniously. This move creates the foundation for the Queen's Gambit family of openings, where we'll soon offer a pawn sacrifice to gain rapid development and central control. The d4 move is less forcing than 1.e4, leading to more positional and strategic games where pawn structure and piece coordination matter tremendously. We're signaling our intention to control the center through pawns first, then bring our pieces into the game with maximum effect.

Black responds symmetrically by occupying the center and contesting our control of the key e4 and c4 squares. This is the most principled response, showing that our opponent wants to maintain central equality rather than allow us free rein in the center. The d5 pawn directly challenges our potential pawn advance to e4 and prepares Black's pieces for natural development. This move sets up the classic Queen's Gambit structure where the central tension will define much of the early middlegame strategy.

We're offering the famous Queen's Gambit, attacking Black's central d5 pawn and trying to deflect it from the center. This isn't a true gambit since we can usually regain the pawn easily, but rather a strategic pawn offer designed to gain time and space in the center. If Black takes with dxc4, we'll have eliminated their central pawn and can later establish a strong pawn center with e4. The move also prepares the development of our queenside pieces, particularly the knight to c3 and eventually the bishop to somewhere useful along the a2-g8 diagonal.

Black accepts the Queen's Gambit, capturing our c4 pawn and temporarily going up material while clearing the d5 square for piece development. This is a perfectly sound decision that leads to sharp, tactical games where Black tries to hold onto the extra pawn while developing quickly. Our opponent is essentially saying 'I'll take your pawn and make you prove you have enough compensation through development and central control.' The Queen's Gambit Accepted often leads to more open, tactical positions compared to the declined variations, which suits players who enjoy concrete calculation over pure positional play.

We're immediately striking back in the center with this powerful advance, establishing a broad pawn center and opening lines for our pieces. This move demonstrates the key point of the Queen's Gambit – by removing Black's d5 pawn, we can now advance our e-pawn two squares with tremendous effect. The e4 pawn controls the crucial f5 and d5 squares, restricts Black's piece development, and opens the diagonal for our light-squared bishop. We're following the classical principle of opening lines and fighting for central space before our opponent can consolidate their position with the extra pawn.

Black develops the knight to its most natural square while immediately attacking our imposing e4 pawn, forcing us to make a concrete decision about our central structure. This is the critical move of the Alekhine System within the Queen's Gambit Accepted – our opponent is developing with tempo by attacking our advanced pawn. The knight on f6 also eyes the important d5 and e4 squares, potentially supporting a future ...e5 advance if we're not careful. From f6, the knight has excellent scope and puts immediate pressure on our ambitious pawn center, testing whether we can maintain our spatial advantage against active piece play.
